title: Records under a mandate: the Rule that governs them
intro: Records are often created and kept because a rule says so — a statute, a regulation, a retention schedule. RiC models that governing rule as a first-class Rule entity, so you can see which records a regulation covers and why they exist. This scenario branches single-file vs series, attaches the agency, and links the governing Rule.
question: How do I record the law or retention rule that governs a set of records?

steps.prompt: A single file, or a series of records governed by the same rule?

choices.label: A single file
choices.why: Good — one file is one Record.

choices.label: A series under one rule
choices.why: Good — records kept under one retention rule form a Record Set, each a member Record.

choices.label: A Rule
choices.why: Not the records. The governing statute/retention schedule is a Rule you relate to the records — modelled in the last step.

choices.label: Record
choices.why: Fits. The file is a Record; the agency is its creating Agent; the statute or schedule that governs it is a related Rule.

choices.label: Record Set
choices.why: Too much for one file — a Set is for the governed series.

choices.label: Mandate
choices.why: Doesn't fit. A Mandate delegates authority between Agents; the file itself is a Record. (The mandate/rule comes later.)

capture.comment: Create the file as a Record.

choices.label: Record Set
choices.why: Fits. Records kept under one rule form a Record Set, each a member Record — and the rule can be related to the set as a whole.

choices.label: Record
choices.why: Undersells it — many files share one retention rule. Use a Record Set.

choices.label: Rule
choices.why: Doesn't fit — the series is the records; the Rule is what governs them.

capture.comment: Create the governed series as a Record Set.

steps.prompt: Attach the agency that created the records.

choices.label: Agency (Corporate Body Agent) + has_creator
choices.why: Fits. The agency is a Corporate Body — an Agent. Relate the records to it with has_creator.

choices.label: The department code in the title
choices.why: Doesn't fit. The agency should be a reconcilable Agent so its whole output is findable.

capture.comment: Create the agency as an Agent.
Link the records to the agency with has_creator.

steps.prompt: Finally — the statute or retention schedule that governs these records. How do you capture it?

choices.label: Rule, linked with is_regulated_by
choices.why: Fits. The governing law or retention schedule is a Rule (RiC-E16). Relate the records to it with is_regulated_by. Now you can answer 'which records does this regulation cover?' and 'under what authority do these records exist / when may they be destroyed?' — the recordkeeping questions a folder can't answer.

choices.label: A retention note on the records
choices.why: Too flat. A rule applies to many record sets and has its own citation, dates and authority; as a Rule entity it links every set it governs.

choices.label: Skip it
choices.why: Misses the recordkeeping dimension. The Rule is what explains why the records exist and how long they're kept — central to regulated records.

capture.comment: Create the governing statute / retention schedule as a Rule.
Link the records to the Rule with is_regulated_by.

outcome.verdict: Records, creator and the rule that governs them.
outcome.summary: The file is a Record (or the series a Record Set); the agency is its creating Agent; and the governing statute/retention schedule is a first-class Rule linked by is_regulated_by. This is RiC's recordkeeping strength — the authority and retention basis for records is explicit and queryable, not buried in a schedule spreadsheet.

links.label: See real RiC data in the graph viewer
